summary: This notebook provides a beginner-friendly tutorial for the waveform inversion competition, demonstrating how to load, visualize, and train a deep learning model to predict subsurface velocity maps from seismic wavefield data. It walks through the complete pipeline: loading `.npy` datasets, visualizing seismic propagation and geological structures, training an InversionNet with L1 loss, and evaluating predictions. The tutorial also covers transfer learning by fine-tuning a model pre-trained on a different geological dataset (CurveVel-A) for the target FlatVel-A domain. Readers should learn how to apply convolutional neural networks to geophysical inverse problems, interpret seismic data formats, and leverage pre-trained weights for domain adaptation.
competition_flow: Loads seismic data and velocity map labels from `.npy` files, trains an InversionNet model using L1 loss to predict velocity maps from seismic waveforms, and evaluates the model on validation data with visualization and transfer learning capabilities.
data_reading: Data and labels are loaded as NumPy arrays from `.npy` files in the competition's `train_samples/FlatVel_A/data/` and `model/` directories.
Velocity maps have shape `(120, 1, 70, 70)` and seismic data has shape `(120, 5, 70, 1000)`.
insights: InversionNet uses a U-Net-like architecture with 2D convolutions and transposed convolutions to map seismic wavefields to velocity maps.
Transfer learning from a curved velocity dataset to a flat velocity dataset is feasible and can be resumed with additional training epochs.
Visualizing seismic wave propagation and velocity structures helps clarify the physical inverse problem setup.
